title: The Problem of Evil: Philosophy's Hardest Question
body:
The problem of evil is the oldest and most persistent challenge to theistic belief: if God is omnipotent (all-powerful), omniscient (all-knowing), and omnibenevolent (all-good), why does suffering exist? Why do children develop terminal illnesses? Why do earthquakes kill thousands? Why does the capacity for cruelty appear to be part of the human endowment?

The logical form of the problem, as sharpened by philosophers from David Hume to J.L. Mackie, claims that these three divine attributes are logically incompatible with the existence of suffering: an omnipotent God could prevent it, an omniscient God would know how, and an omnibenevolent God would want to. Since suffering exists, at least one of the three attributes must be false — which means the God of classical theism does not exist.

The major responses — theodicies, as they are called — attempt to explain why an omnipotent, omniscient, omnibenevolent God might have reasons to permit suffering that we cannot fully perceive. The free will defence argues that genuine freedom requires the real possibility of wrong choice, and that a world containing free creatures who can choose evil is better than a world of determined creatures who cannot. The soul-making theodicy (associated with John Hick) argues that suffering is the necessary context for the development of virtues — courage, compassion, endurance — that give life its deepest meaning.

Neither response fully satisfies its critics. The free will defence addresses moral evil (evil produced by human choices) but not natural evil (earthquakes, disease). The soul-making theodicy seems to require an implausibly large quantity of suffering to produce its educational benefits. The problem remains genuinely hard — which is part of what makes it philosophically important.

title: Buddhist Philosophy and the Self: What It Actually Teaches About Consciousness
body:
The Buddhist doctrine of anatta — no-self — is among the most radical and most misunderstood propositions in the history of philosophy. It does not claim that you do not exist. It claims that what you take yourself to be — a unified, persistent self that is the subject of experience and the owner of consciousness — is a conceptual construction rather than a metaphysical reality.

The Buddhist analysis of what appears to be the self identifies five aggregates: form (the physical body and its sense organs), sensation (the felt quality of experience), perception (the recognition of objects), mental formations (volitions, emotions, habits), and consciousness (awareness). None of these, individually or collectively, constitutes a permanent, unified self. What we call 'I' is a process — a constantly changing flow of interdependent physical and mental events — that the mind habituates to labelling as a thing.

The practical implications the Buddha drew from this analysis are specific: if the self is a construction rather than a fixed reality, then the suffering produced by defending and aggrandising that self is unnecessary. Liberation — nirvana — is not the annihilation of the self but the end of the compulsive clinging to a self that never existed as we imagined it.

Contemporary philosophy of mind has approached similar territory through entirely different routes. Derek Parfit's work on personal identity — particularly his conclusion that personal identity is not what matters, and that what matters is psychological continuity — reaches conclusions strikingly similar to Buddhist anatta. The convergence between ancient contemplative analysis and contemporary analytic philosophy suggests that both are tracking something real about the nature of consciousness and identity.

title: Existentialism: What Sartre, Camus, and Beauvoir Actually Believed
body:
Existentialism — the philosophical movement centred on the individual's freedom, responsibility, and confrontation with existence — has generated more distortion than perhaps any other philosophical school. The distortions typically run in two directions: a romantic distortion (existentialism as the philosophy of dark coffee and brooding in Paris) and a nihilistic distortion (existentialism as the view that nothing matters). Neither is accurate.

Sartre's central claim — 'existence precedes essence' — means that human beings, unlike manufactured objects, do not arrive with a predetermined purpose or nature. A table is made for something; its essence (what it is for) precedes its existence. A human being simply exists first, and then creates their essence through their choices and commitments. This is not cause for despair but for responsibility: we are radically free, and that freedom is inescapable.

Albert Camus, often associated with existentialism though he rejected the label, developed a complementary position around the concept of the absurd: the conflict between the human desire for meaning and the universe's silence on the question. His response to the absurd was not nihilism but revolt — the decision to live fully and passionately in the face of meaninglessness, to embrace the absurd as the condition of an authentic life.

Simone de Beauvoir applied existentialist principles to ethics and feminism, arguing that the freedom that existentialism demands must be pursued not only for oneself but for all others — that genuine freedom is incompatible with the oppression of others, and that the liberation of women was a prerequisite for any authentic human freedom. Her contribution to existentialism is both philosophically substantial and historically undervalued.

title: Mysticism Across Traditions: What the Contemplatives Agree On
body:
The perennial philosophy — the idea that beneath the enormous diversity of the world's religious traditions there is a common core of mystical experience — was articulated in modern form by Aldous Huxley but has a much older history. The observation that Christian mystics, Sufi masters, Buddhist contemplatives, Jewish Kabbalists, and Hindu sages describe strikingly similar experiences of unity, selflessness, and direct encounter with the ground of reality has impressed thinkers from Leibniz to William James.

The common features are specific. The experience of unity — the dissolution of the ordinary sense of separation between self and world, self and other, self and the ultimate reality — is the most consistently reported feature. The ineffability of the experience: it cannot be adequately described in ordinary language. The noetic quality: it carries a sense of having directly perceived something true about the nature of reality. The transience: the experience does not persist, though its effects may last a lifetime. The sense of sacredness or presence.

Sceptics have argued that the apparent similarity of mystical experiences across traditions is an artifact of interpretation — that what is actually universal is the category of 'mystical experience', which then gets interpreted through the conceptual frameworks of each tradition. The Christian mystic experiences God; the Buddhist meditator experiences sunyata (emptiness); the Hindu yogi experiences Brahman. These may be the same experience described in different vocabularies, or they may be genuinely different experiences that share surface similarities.

The question matters beyond academic interest: if the contemplative traditions do converge on a common reality, it suggests that human consciousness has genuine access to dimensions of existence that ordinary awareness does not reveal — a possibility with significant implications for how we understand the nature of mind.

title: Free Will and Determinism: Does Your Choice Actually Matter?
body:
The free will debate is not an abstract philosophical puzzle. Its answer determines whether moral responsibility, guilt, punishment, and praise are rationally justified. If our choices are entirely determined by prior causes — our genes, our upbringing, our neural states at the moment of decision — then in what sense are we responsible for them? And if we are not responsible, what justifies treating people differently based on the choices they make?

Hard determinism argues that all events, including human choices, are the inevitable products of prior causes operating according to physical laws. The feeling of freedom — of genuinely having been able to choose otherwise — is an illusion produced by our inability to introspect our own causal processes. This position is internally consistent but has consequences that many find unacceptable: it appears to undermine the rational basis for moral responsibility.

Libertarian free will (not to be confused with the political position) argues that human choices are genuinely undetermined — that some property of human consciousness (consciousness itself, perhaps, or quantum indeterminacy) allows for genuine origination of choice not fully explained by prior causes. This view preserves moral responsibility but faces the challenge of explaining how a random or undetermined choice is more free than a determined one.

Compatibilism — the dominant position among contemporary philosophers — argues that free will and determinism are compatible, because 'free will' properly understood does not require the ability to have done otherwise, but rather the ability to act in accordance with one's own desires and reasons without external coercion. On this view, you act freely when you do what you want to do, even if your wanting is itself determined. Critics argue that this is a redefinition of free will rather than a solution to the problem.

title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence: What Philosophy Has to Say
body:
The development of artificial intelligence presents philosophy with some of its most urgent practical questions. What moral status, if any, should be accorded to AI systems? Who bears responsibility when an AI system causes harm? How should the benefits and risks of AI be distributed? Can a machine have genuine interests that deserve moral consideration?

The question of moral status depends on the criteria we use to assign it. Sentience-based views — associated with Peter Singer and utilitarian ethics — hold that the capacity to suffer is the relevant criterion. If an AI system can genuinely suffer (a question on which there is no current scientific consensus), it would warrant moral consideration on this view. Rational agency-based views — associated with Kantian ethics — hold that the capacity for rational autonomous choice is the criterion. Whether current AI systems meet this standard is similarly contested.

The responsibility question is practically urgent: when a self-driving vehicle causes a fatal accident, or when a medical diagnostic AI misses a cancer, who is accountable? Existing legal and moral frameworks for responsibility were developed in a world of human agents making human decisions. They do not map neatly onto systems in which decisions emerge from the interaction of training data, model architecture, deployment context, and the choices of users who may be distant from the development process.

The distributive question is perhaps the most immediately consequential: the productivity gains from AI will accrue primarily to those who own the systems, while the displacement costs will be borne primarily by those whose labour the systems replace. Classical philosophical frameworks — from Rawlsian justice to utilitarian welfare economics — provide different and sometimes incompatible guidance on how these gains and costs should be shared.
